agent1_snippets.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Function
agent1_snippets.content: The last major type of T cells is the T regulatory cell (also known as suppressor T cells). These cells modulate immune responses and inhibit autoimmune processes (such as autoreactive immune cells). T regulatory cells produce inhibitory IL-10 and IL-35 cytokines to control immune responses, as well as using CTLA-4 to inhibit B7 on antigen-presenting cells to decrease immune responses. Production of T regulatory cells is spurred by cytokine IL-2 (so much so that in autoimmune disease IL-2 is often absent) and TGF-beta. T regulatory cells express the TCR (CD3), CD4 (they likely share a common lineage with CD4+ Helper T cells), CTLA-4 and CD25. They also express the biomarker FOXP3 (a transcription marker important in their development and immune functions). There has been a recent interest in T regulatory cells as a method to promote wound healing after surgery and to battle cancers. [14] [15]

agent1_snippets.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Anatomy and Physiology
agent1_snippets.content: Several cellular and molecular mechanisms explain the beneficial effects of immunotherapy including allergic specific suppression of inducible CD4(+), CD 25+, forkhead box p3+ T-regulatory cells, and IL-10 secreting T-regulatory cells, preventing their increase in peripheral blood. Other mechanisms include suppression of eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils, and the switching of antibodies from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies. [9] [10] [11] Other mechanisms, including a switch from a TH2 to a THI  immune deviation,  and other changes in humoral/cellular immunity, are still being elucidated. [10] [12] [13] [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyradiculoneuropathy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Once clinicians establish efficacy, IVIG therapy stops until the patient experiences clinical deterioration. The corresponding interval determines their future treatment interval. The patient will then be given the IVIG with a subsequent 20% reduction in dose each time until a final dosing schedule is determined. The typical dose is 1.4 g/kg at a mean interval of 4.3 weeks, but some patients can go as low as 0.4mg/kg. [36] The immunomodulating mechanisms of IVIG can be summarized as follows: [37] Inhibition of innate immune cell activation Neutralization of autoantibodies Inhibition and abrogation of activated complement Induction of autophagy in peripheral blood mononuclear cells Modulation of fragment crystallizable receptors (FcR) Decrease of T helper 17 (Th17) cell proliferation and interleukin 17 (IL-17) secretion Decrease in proinflammatory cytokine production Increase in regulatory T-cells (Treg) expansion

agent1_snippets.content: IPEX occurs as a result of a mutation in the FOXP3 transcription factor. FOXP3 is crucial for the function of Treg cells also known as regulatory T lymphocyte. Treg cells have a strong immune suppressive effect and serve a function in establishing immune balance. Moreover, they are involved in transplantation tolerance as well as in the regulation of autoimmune and allergic events.

agent1_snippets.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging and Future Immunotherapy Targets
agent1_snippets.content: Several combinatorial strategies have been proposed to have shown promise in developing future therapeutic strategies against tumors that show resistance to currently available strategies. The Ang/Tie 2 pathway is thought to be responsible for basal angiogenesis and vascular stability following vascular endothelial growth factor blockade. [126] Inhibitors such as trebananib, which block this novel pathway, may have a role in combination with VEGF blockade. [127] ALK 1 inhibitors, such as dalantercept, may have a role in interfering with the formation of the vascular bed. [128] Dual mTOR c1/c2 inhibitors may be a potential therapeutic option since the existing therapies only target them on the TOR c1 pathway. [129] Inhibitors targeting hypoxia-inducible factor 2 and aberrant glycolysis, aberrant glutamine, and tryptophan metabolisms, such as CB 839 (glutamine metabolism) indoleamine 2.3 dioxygenase inhibitors, are being developed with an intent to lead to potential additional benefit. [130] [131] It has been suggested that the relationship between the tumor and it’s immune micro-environment needs to be studied further, with the intent to developing beneficial combinatorial therapy options. Other studied approaches include personalized vaccination, targeted radiotherapy to enhance immune responses, cytoreductive surgery following systemic therapy, and immunotherapy or targeted therapy in the neoadjuvant setting. [132] [133] Several molecules that are being investigated as potential targets include chemokine receptors, lymphocyte activation gene – 3, OX -40 (CD 134), B and T lymphocyte attenuator (BTLA), and V-domain containing immunoglobulin containing suppressor of T – lymphocytes activation, soluble. [134]

agent1_snippets.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT). [27] The house dust mite allergenic extracts are injected in an incrementally increasing dose into the affected individual. The therapeutic dose range of SCIT is between 500 and 2000 AU (activity units) for a targeted maintenance dose of 7 micrograms of Der p 1 and 10 micrograms of Der f 1. The build-up or the induction phase is about 8 - 24 weeks, followed by 3 to 5 years of a monthly maintenance phase. SCIT induces “allergenic tolerance.” The mechanism involves the induction of FOXP3+ CD25+ regulatory T cells (Tregs) specific to such allergens, induction of blocking antibodies such as IgG4 and IgA2, as well as induction of regulatory cytokines such TGF-beta and IL-10  that skew the response from a TH2 to a Treg or TH1 pattern. It provides an effective improvement in clinical symptoms, reduces the need for rescue medications, and improves lung function.

agent1_snippets.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Helper T cells
agent1_snippets.content: Despite the acknowledged importance of CD4 + T cells in herpes stromal keratitis's immunopathogenesis, the specificity of their antigen recognition is still debatable. Some research suggests these T cells may respond to autoantigens exposed during infection. However, this hypothesis faces skepticism due to the lack of observed cross-reactivity with presumed autoantigens and the absence of a correlation with disease severity, challenging the molecular mimicry theory. An alternative explanation, bystander activation, gained traction following observations that reconstituted athymic mice with T cells nonspecific to viral antigens still developed stromal lesions and failed to clear the infection, leading to encephalitis. This nuanced understanding of CD4 + T cell roles, from Th1 and Th17 cell-mediated inflammation to Treg-mediated immunoregulation, underscores the complexity of immune responses in stromal keratitis. Further research into the antigen specificity of CD4 + T cells and their interactions within the corneal environment is essential for developing targeted therapies for herpes stromal keratitis. [40]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
agent2_snippets.content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes inhibitory interleukin agents, the antimetabolite mycophenolate (also known as mycophenolic acid) and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including its hydrochloride form), as well as azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine, everolimus, gliotoxin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus. Mycophenolate and mycophenolic acid function by blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes, whereas cyclosporine and tacrolimus inhibit calcineurin, preventing activation of T‑cell transcription factors. Everolimus exerts its effect through inhibition of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, limiting cell proliferation. Basiliximab and muromonab‑CD3 are monoclonal antibodies that target specific surface receptors on activated T cells, providing targeted immunosuppression. Gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, also possesses immunosuppressive properties through interference with cytokine signaling. Collectively, these agents illustrate the multiple mechanistic pathways—interleukin inhibition, antimetabolite activity, calcineurin blockade, mTOR inhibition, and monoclonal antibody‑mediated receptor blockade—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their central role in contemporary patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1485
agent2_snippets.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad therapeutic class used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes agents that act through diverse mechanisms, ranging from inhibition of interleukin signaling to blockade of calcineurin or mTOR pathways and direct depletion of lymphocytes. Representative members of the immunosuppressant family are inhibitory interleukinu (agents that inhibit interleukin activity), mycophenolate, and mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil). Additional compounds classified as immunosuppressants are antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum), azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny (cyclosporine), daklizumab (daclizumab), elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, and hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil). Specific immunosuppressive drugs also highlighted within this group are kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus (tacrolimus). By recognizing the wide array of immunosuppressants and their distinct pharmacologic actions, clinicians can tailor immunomodulatory regimens to achieve optimal graft survival, control autoimmune activity, and minimize adverse effects.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
agent2_snippets.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad class of agents that dampen immune activity through a variety of mechanisms and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. The group includes inhibitory interleukin agents, antimetabolites such as mycophenolate, mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), azaserin, and koformycin, which interfere with nucleotide synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation. Calcineurin inhibitors, exemplified by cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus), block T‑cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin signaling. The mTOR pathway is targeted by everolimus, providing another route to suppress cell growth and immune responses. Monoclonal antibodies and biologic agents such as basiliximab, daklizumab (daclizumab), muromonab‑CD3, and elipticiny act on specific surface receptors or cytokine pathways to prevent lymphocyte activation. Polyclonal preparations like antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) also contribute to immunosuppression by broadly neutralizing immune cells. Glucocorticoids, including meprednisone and paramethason (paramethasone), exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by altering gene transcription. Additionally, gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, possesses immunosuppressive properties through inhibition of NF‑κB and other signaling cascades. Together, these diverse agents illustrate the multiple biochemical targets—interleukin signaling, calcineurin, mTOR, nucleotide synthesis, surface receptors, and transcriptional pathways—through which immune function can be attenuated, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens and managing associated risks.

Within this class, basiliximab, everolimus and gliotoxin are recognized as specific immunosuppressive agents. Additional agents classified as immunosuppressants include koformycin, the mycophenolic acid derivative known as kyselina mykofenolová, the corticosteroid meprednisone, the monoclonal antibody muromonab‑CD3, the corticosteroid paramethason, and the calcineurin inhibitor takrolimus. It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400

Within this class, inhibitory interleukin agents are considered a subordinate subgroup (“has child”). These agents act to dampen immune responses and are employed in conditions requiring suppression of lymphocyte activity, such as organ transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and certain inflammatory disorders. However, the use of immunosuppressants is contraindicated in the setting of human herpesvirus 1 (HSV‑1) infection, reflecting the heightened risk of viral reactivation when immune function is deliberately reduced. Recognizing both the therapeutic breadth of immunosuppressants and their specific contraindications is essential for safe patient management and optimal clinical outcomes.

agent2_snippets.content: Biosynthesis, the cellular process of constructing complex molecules from simpler precursors, can be qualified or modulated by a diverse set of agents—including the antibiotic‑like compound mepartricin, the amino acid cystein, the neurosteroid pregnanolone, the prostacyclin analog epoprostenol, the alkaloid protoveratriny, the quinone glaukarubin, the ionophore lasalocid, the steroid ester testosteronpropionát, and simple carbohydrates such as trisacharidy—as well as by several immunologically active substances such as cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, gliotoxin, koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, and inhibitory interleukins, each of which influences specific enzymatic, signaling, or ion‑dependent steps of metabolic pathways. Many of these qualifiers are themselves immunosuppressive agents; the immunosuppressant class encompasses antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daklizumab, elipticiny, everolimus, gliotoxin, koformycin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Production pathways of certain immunosuppressive preparations—specifically antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin and basiliximab—are directly linked to biosynthetic routes, underscoring the metabolic origins of these drugs. Within the immunosuppressant hierarchy, inhibitory interleukins form a child subgroup that dampens immune signaling. Because immunosuppressants reduce host immune defenses, their use is contraindicated in patients with active lidský herpesvirus 1 (human herpesvirus 1) infection, highlighting the need for infection screening before therapy. Understanding how these varied compounds qualify biosynthesis and how they intersect with immunosuppressive pharmacology is essential for optimizing therapeutic strategies, anticipating toxicological effects, and guiding patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1734
agent2_snippets.content: Antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyklosporiny, daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3 and takrolimus are all classified as immunosuppressive agents. These drugs share the common function of dampening immune system activity, which makes them valuable in settings such as organ transplantation, where preventing graft rejection is essential, and in the treatment of certain autoimmune disorders. Antilymfocytární sérum and muromonab‑CD3 act as antibodies that target lymphocytes, while basiliximab, daclizumab and takrolimus inhibit T‑cell activation through distinct molecular pathways. Cyklosporiny and everolimus belong to the calcineurin‑inhibitor and mTOR‑inhibitor families, respectively, providing synergistic suppression of cellular immunity. Azaserin, gliotoxin, koformycin and kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) exert their effects by interfering with nucleic acid synthesis or other intracellular processes critical for lymphocyte proliferation. Together, this diverse group of immunosuppressants offers clinicians multiple mechanisms to tailor therapy according to the specific immunologic needs of each patient, thereby improving graft survival and disease control while balancing the risk of infection and other adverse effects.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
agent2_snippets.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1722
agent2_snippets.content: Immunosuppressants are a class of immunologic factors that act to dampen or inhibit the activity of the immune system. Within this class, several agents are identified as direct “children,” including inhibitory interleukin agents, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). A broad range of specific drugs and biologic preparations are also classified as immunosuppressants: antilymphocyte serum, cyclosporine (cyklosporiny), daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, and the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). Additional individual compounds that belong to the immunosuppressant group are mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, paramethasone, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Collectively, these agents are employed to prevent graft rejection in organ transplantation, to treat autoimmune disorders, and to manage other conditions where suppression of immune activity is therapeutically desirable. Their shared classification under immunosuppressants underscores a common mechanism of reducing immune‑mediated damage while highlighting the diversity of chemical structures and clinical applications within the group.

agent2_snippets.content: Everolimus, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid), meprednisone, paramethason, and takrolimus are all members of the immunosuppressant drug class. As immunosuppressants, each of these agents functions to dampen the activity of the immune system and is commonly employed in settings such as organ transplantation and the treatment of autoimmune disorders. Although they share the overarching purpose of immune suppression, they act through distinct mechanisms: everolimus inhibits the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, takrolimus (tacrolimus) blocks calcineurin, kyselina mykofenolová interferes with inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase to reduce lymphocyte proliferation, and both meprednisone and paramethason are corticosteroids that modulate inflammatory gene expression. Recognizing that these diverse compounds belong to the immunosuppressant category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens, anticipating drug‑specific side effects, and optimizing patient care.
